data.title: Beta FinTech Targets $10 Million U.S. IPO Despite Worsening Results
data.description:
Beta FinTech’s Financial Net Results Are Deteriorating

Beta FinTech Holdings Limited (BTFT) has filed to raise $10 million in an IPO of its ordinary shares, according to SEC F-1 registration information.

BTFT provides a range of financial and related services in Hong Kong.

Given the numerous risks the company poses, its tiny size, thin capitalization, high valuation expectations and worsening net results, my outlook on the BTFT IPO is to sell (avoid it).

What Does Beta FinTech Do?

Hong Kong, China-based Beta FinTech Holdings Limited was founded to develop various service offerings to companies operating in the PRC.

Management is led by Chief Executive Officer Mr. Xianxin Xiang, who has been with the firm since 2024 and was previously CEO of FTFT International Securities and Futures Limited.

The company's primary offerings include:

  • Brokerage services

  • Placing

  • Underwriting

  • Financial advisory

  • Margin loans

  • Other.

In the most recent six-month period, the company has generated revenue contribution per this pie chart:

Beta FinTech has booked fair market value investment of $7.2 million as of December 31, 2024 from investors, including Jieying International Holdings, Real Wisdom Capital (CEO Xiang controls), Amazing Genius International, Cong Gao and WellCell Tech [HK] Co.

What Is Beta FinTech’s Market?

According to a 2024 market research report by HKTDC, the Hong Kong financial services industry accounted for nearly 25% of the city's GDP in 2023.

It also represented 7.3% of total employment.

The region hosts the largest pool of RMB liquidity outside mainland China.

Also, it is therefore the largest offshore clearing center for RMB globally.

The company faces intense competition from hundreds of small, medium and large-scale international companies.

The firm assists companies seeking to go public on the KEX as well as provides other related securities dealing and financial advisory services.

It generates revenue through a combination of percentage commissions, fees and interest from margin loans.

Beta FinTech’s IPO Plan

BTFT intends to sell two million ordinary shares at a proposed midpoint price of $5.00 per share for gross proceeds of approximately $10.0 million, not including the sale of customary underwriter options.

No existing or possibly new shareholders have indicated an interest in acquiring additional shares, at least as disclosed in the prospectus.

Dilution in net tangible book value per share for investors in the IPO is expected to be $3.38.

The company’s enterprise value at IPO (assuming no underwriter options) will approximate $102 million.

The float-to-outstanding-shares ratio (excluding underwriter options) will be approximately 9.85%, so the stock will be a ‘low float’ stock subject to potentially higher volatility in open market trading.

According to Beta FinTech’s most recent regulatory filing, it plans to use the net proceeds as follows:

$3.2 million, which is expected to be approximately 50% of the net proceeds, for the updating and improvement of our current product offerings, including to hire more employees and to improve and expand our online trading platform to more markets, and to expand the types of services offered to our customers;

$1.3 million, which is expected to be approximately 20% of the net proceeds, for the applications for brokerage licenses to be made to the relevant regulators in the United States and the following Southeast Asian countries: Malaysia, Thailand, and Vietnam. Specifically, the fees and expenses related to obtaining a brokerage license in the United States is about $0.9 million, and the fees and expenses related to obtaining a brokerage license in a Southeast Asian country (either Thailand, Indonesia, or Malaysia), would be about $0.4 million; and

$1.8 million, which is expected to be approximately 30% of the net proceeds, for operating purposes, including as capital required to provide margin financing to customers, and for marketing and business development efforts to increase revenue.

(Source - SEC.)

The firm’s presentation of the company roadshow is not available.

Pertaining to potential legal exposure, company leadership said the firm is not currently the subject of any litigation that would have a material adverse effect on its financial condition or operating results.

Beta FinTech Is A Small Company With Worsening Financial Results

BTFT is seeking U.S. public capital market funding for its growth plans within Hong Kong and outside the region.

The firm’s financials have produced growing topline revenue from a tiny base, fluctuating operating profit, falling operating margin but increasing cash flow from operations.

Free cash flow for the twelve months ended December 31, 2024, was negative ($2.3 million).

Selling and Marketing expenses as a percentage of total revenue have grown as revenue has increased from a small base and its Selling and Marketing efficiency multiple fell to 11.9x in the most recent reporting period.

The firm currently plans to pay no dividends and will likely retain any future earnings and cash flows to reinvest back into its growth and working capital needs.

BTFT’s recent capital spending history shows it has continued to spend on capital expenditures despite generating negative operating cash flow.

The market opportunity for providing financial services in Hong Kong is large and will likely grow, but the market features intense competition from companies of all sizes.

Risks to the company’s outlook as a public firm include its "foreign private issuer" and "emerging growth company" status, which enable management to reduce the amount of information it provides to shareholders.

Also, the firm is subject to unpredictable and arbitrary regulatory risks operating in Hong Kong.

BTFT also has significant customer concentration risks, with three clients accounting for 63% of its revenue for the six months ended December 31, 2024.

Beta also has numerous related party transactions, as detailed in the prospectus here.

Beta FinTech is seeking a Price/Sales multiple that is far higher than the U.S. public financial services company benchmark, although its EV/EBITDA multiple is somewhat lower.

Given the numerous risks the company poses, its tiny size, thin capitalization, high valuation expectations and worsening net results, my outlook on the BTFT IPO is to sell (avoid it).

Expected IPO Pricing Date: To be announced.

data.title: Federal Reserve Watch: Money Is Available
data.description:
The times are starting to catch up.

I have been writing over the past year or so about the fact that the Federal Reserve pumped billions of dollars...even trillions...into the banking system to protect the U.S. economy from the COVID-19 disaster.

A lot of this money was not spent as the velocity of circulation of the money stock took a major dip.

Here take a look at what occurred back then.

During the year 2020, the M2 money stock expanded by around 24 percent.

The use of that money, represented by the velocity of circulation of the money stock, dropped off precipitously.

Consequently, the United States did not experience the amount of inflation that would usually accompany this amount of increase in the money stock.

As things settled down in the economy, the M2 money stock tapered off in 2021 and 2022.

The velocity of circulation of the M2 money stock began to rise.

Currently, we see that the M2 money stock has been rising again, and it has now grown to where it exceeds the level it reached in the Spring of 2022.

Analysts have been pointing to this turnaround and have called attention to how this expansion might lead to growing inflation in the United States.

I think that there is greater concern that needs to be expressed just by looking at the figures from January 2022 to the present. I think that we need to go back to January 2000 and really worry about all the money that the Federal Reserve has injected into the banking system since the fight against COVID-19 really started.

Here is the time period I think we should be worried about.

As far as the "use" of money is concerned, the velocity of circulation of the M2 money has returned to about where it was before the beginning of the battle against COVID-19 affects.

But, the velocity of circulation could go higher.

Let's look at what M2 velocity was doing before 2020.

The M2 velocity of circulation had been declining...steadily...before 2020.

So, it is highly possible for the M2 velocity of circulation to move to higher levels in the near future if inflation begins picking up...a real possibility.

This is the environment the Federal Reserve faces. The Federal Reserve must be extremely worried not only about what has happened since early in 2024 when the M2 money stock began to rise once again, but also extremely worried about what happened beginning in early 2020.

For three and one-half years, the Federal Reserve has been reducing the size of its securities portfolio to bring down the number of reserves that exist within the banking system and control the size of the money stock.

It has done a terrific job...so far.

But, there are still a lot of excess reserves in the commercial banking system, almost $3.4 trillion.

These "excess" reserves could be used. The M2 money stock could grow faster.

President Trump could get his wish of faster money stock growth and lower interest rates.

The major point here is that the Federal Reserve would not have to pump a lot more reserves into the commercial banking system.

THE EXCESS RESERVES ARE ALREADY THERE!!!!!

The position of Fed Chairman Jerome Powell and the Fed's FOMC is that the Fed has more work to do.

Excess reserves must be reduced further.

The commercial banking system still has "too much" cash in its vaults.

The fact that the M2 money stock is growing faster now and that the velocity of circulation is rising is an indication that the "excess reserves" of the commercial banking system are being put to work.

Trump does not have to have the Federal Reserve "pump" more reserves into the commercial banking system to get the liquidity for the economic expansion he wants to create.

The reserves are already in the banking system, they just need to be "drawn upon."

There is some indication in the system that people are now drawing on them faster than they were, say, six months ago.

And, this is what Mr. Powell and the Federal Reserve are worried about.

How can we tell that the "cash" is being used?

If the M2 money stock continues to rise and if the M2 velocity of circulation continues to rise...then we have a pretty good idea that the "cash" is being used.

However, this will also be a sign to me that we are moving in the wrong direction.

To me, the Federal Reserve needs to continue to reduce the size of its securities portfolio. That it is, the "excess reserves" in the commercial banking system still need to be reduced, and the amount it needs to be reduced is still a pretty big number.

As far as the actual practice going on.....

In the banking week ending June 4, 2025, the Fed's portfolio of securities held outright dropped by $1.6 billion.

Since the current round of quantitative tightening began, September 18, 2024, the Fed has overseen a reduction in its securities portfolio of $315.5 billion. In addition, loans by the Fed to the commercial banking system have fallen by $94.0 billion.

Since March 16, 2022, when the program of quantitative easing began, the securities portfolio of the Federal Reserve has been reduced by $2.1 trillion.

Reserve Balances with Federal Reserve banks, on June 4, 2025, a "proxy" for the excess reserves in the commercial banking system, totaled $3.4 trillion.

The Federal Reserve, in my mind, still has a long journey to travel until it returns to a more "normal" level of "cash" in the banking system. Therefore, from my viewpoint, the banking system still remains the source of a possible surge in future rates of inflation.

Trump does not need a new Fed chairman to "open the spigots." The money is already there.

data.title: Eaton: A Dip In Stock Price Would Serve Investors Well
data.description:
Company Description

Eaton Corporation plc (NYSE:ETN) is a worldwide power management company with activity in the electrical, aerospace, and vehicle sectors. The company operates through five segments, Electrical Americas, Electrical Global, Aerospace, Vehicle, and eMobility. ETN sports a current market cap of nearly $130B and employs close to 100,000 people. The stock has performed very well over the past decade, achieving a total return of almost 500%, or a CAGR of 19.5%.

Quality Financial Metrics

Let's assess some important quantitative financial metrics for ETN to determine how the company has performed.

Eaton's revenue per share has grown at a clip of 3.8% over the past ten years. For the most part, this metric grew steadily between FY15 and FY19, before a 13% decline in FY20. Following that double-digit decrease, this metric rebounded, with increases in the high-single digit to low-double digit range since. The company has a share buyback program and typically reduces its share count by about 1% per year, with some years better than others.

The gross profit margin oscillated between 30.5% and 38.2% over the past decade, with a total increase of more than 20% during that timeframe. There was an unsurprising decline in FY20, but this metric has since risen steadily, including peaking at 38.2% in the most recent fiscal year.

The return on invested capital for ETN has followed a similar path as the gross profit margin. This metric was generally on an upward trend for the first half of the chart before dropping to 5% in 2020. Following the bottom-out in FY20 the ROIC has bounced back with increases every year since.

Dividend Data

Eaton pays a quarterly dividend of $1.04 per share or $4.16 annually which equates to a current dividend yield of 1.28%. ETN offers some inflation-beating dividend growth, with its 3-year dividend growth rate slightly higher than 8%, while its 5 & 10-year dividend growth rates are north of 6.5%. Aside from 2020, the payout ratio has averaged about 50% over the past ten years and has been declining recently. It's definitely an encouraging sign for dividend investors like myself to see the payout ratio dropping which will hopefully lead to healthy dividend increases in the future.

Q1 2025 Earnings

In early May, Eaton announced their Q1 2025 earnings with a double beat, Non-GAAP EPS was $2.72, topping expectations by $0.01 on revenue of $6.4B which also exceeded the consensus by $150M and was a 7.3% increase year-over-year. Additionally, GAAP EPS was $2.45 surpassing analyst estimates by $0.02.

Looking at the segments individually, we'll start with the Electrical Americas segment which saw revenue surge by 12% to $3B for the quarter, with organic sales up 13%, partially offset by exchange rate headwinds. Furthermore, operating profit for this segment was $904M, a 15% increase from Q1 2024.

The Electrical Global segment of the business had sales of $1.6B, a 7% increase from Q1 2024. This segment faced similar currency headwinds but also had organic sales rise 9%.

Moving on to the Aerospace segment, sales were up 12% to nearly $1B. Additionally, organic sales climbed 13%, while operating earnings followed suit rising 12% for the quarter to a record $226M.

The Vehicle portion of the business struggled mightily with revenue dropping 15% YoY to $617M. The operating margin for the quarter was 15.5%, while the operating profit was a tick below $100M.

Last but not least is the eMobility segment which had a modest 2% increase in sales to $162M. However, this segment earned an operating loss for the quarter due to costs related to new initiatives which management expects to see benefits from in the coming quarters.

Management also reaffirmed its FY25 outlook where they project organic growth in the mid-to-high-single-digits; as well as GAAP EPS growth of 10% at the midpoint ($10.49), with adjusted EPS around $12.00 per share or an increase of 11% YoY.

The stock was rather mute immediately following the earnings report; however, since then, it has risen nearly 11%. The full earnings call can be read/listened to here.

Valuation

The final piece of this puzzle is to determine if ETN is trading for an attractive valuation, and to do so we're going to look at it from a free cash flow perspective. Additionally, to give a little more context on how to view the chart below, the estimated fair value is calculated by dividing the FCF/share for that year by the trailing 5-year average of the FCF yield. The discount line is 10% below the estimated FV, while the premium is 10% above, and finally, the Trim line is 40% above the approximate FV. One final note, you'll notice the chart goes into 2025, and because no complete 2025 Free Cash Flow per share data is available, the trailing twelve-month free cash flow per share is used.

My custom valuation model suggests ETN is trading for a premium at the moment, with a fair price closer to $243. Near the beginning of the chart, the stock traded for a modest discount, before trading for a significant premium in the years following the pandemic. The stock has continued to trade for a premium since 2020, rarely dipping below the trim level.

The projected long-term rate of return for Eaton comes in at almost 7%, with the components of that estimate as follows; a dividend yield of 1.28%, a return to fair value factor of -6.03%, and an expected earnings growth rate of 11.70%. I usually aim for a forward rate of return closer to 10%, so this is somewhat discouraging, although the overvaluation is essentially the root cause.

Final Thoughts

ETN stock has performed well compared to the broad market over the past decade, with multiple years earning a total return exceeding 40%. The company has some decent financial metrics that all appear to be trending higher. The dividend yield isn't anything amazing, but the dividend growth is encouraging, and typically better than inflation. Moreover, aside from the Vehicle segment, all other segments of the business appear to be growing with no signs of slowing down. The one drawback I see here is the valuation, with the stock consistently trading for a premium, investors may be hard-pressed to find a good entry point. Thus, I rate the stock a HOLD, I wouldn't be initiating a position at these levels, but if you bought in at a lower price point I certainly wouldn't be exiting my position anytime soon.

data.title: Salesforce: A Beta Play Delivering Excess Value
data.description:
Salesforce (CRM) is frequently mentioned as a cheap or undervalued stock on account of its depressed pricing multiples. During my analysis I outline why this is not the case, and why the stock is trading at around a two-year premium. Despite this, I view Salesforce entering the stage of moving to increased profitability, and thereby increasing the value for investors. While I see limitations in growth, including M&A driven-growth, I view that the main value factor for the company will be the increase in GAAP operating margins in the future, which I mark as the KPI for investors following the company. Competitors are a main risk factor for the company as the tech becomes easier to replicate. However, the main moat for Salesforce is their established enterprise-level customer base, which is sticky on account of the difficulty in replacing the large system that is Salesforce. The company has higher up-front costs for customer acquisition but can minimize the maintenance costs for retention, which will be the key driver that allows for increased margins over time.

I value Salesforce around $200B, or $204 per share, with a 1-year target price of $230 per share with a positive outlook as I expect the company to become more valuable as it returns 13% p.a. to investors, in excess of my estimated required return of 8.5%.

While stocks are forward-looking, popular names tend to be tied to the near-ish term, i.e. to projections 2-3 years out. This is especially true for stocks that have a stabilized TAM, such as Salesforce or Google. In situations like this, the question is how much will the TAM grow, as opposed to how large it is. In contrast, companies like Palantir and Cloudflare are being speculated on at the TAM-level, where investors try to estimate just how large the target industry is.

Because of this, I view that analyzing Salesforce comes down to the growth of digitalization of the economy, as well as how much can Salesforce charge their customers [enterprise managers] for the privilege of making the “safe choice” when it comes to buying customer relationship management [CRM] software.

Setting Growth Expectations For Salesforce

For Salesforce, Q1 was the first time [since 2019] that the company saw a QoQ revenue top by posting $9.83B, compared to the almost $10B in the previous quarter. Upper revenue guidance for FY '26 was around $41.3B, implying an 8.7% growth.

Growth has been slowly trending down as the company increased its size, and while this is expected, it seems that the AI growth story isn’t enough to offset the lack of organic software growth opportunities.

The chart below illustrates the growth curve for the company on a trailing 12-month basis.

As growth decelerates, the question becomes “can Salesforce revitalize with AI, or will it need another avenue?”

While the AI growth story is compelling, to me, it looks more like a defensive CapEx spend driven by broad AI adoption across the field. It’s not so much that CRM offers a unique customer advantage with AI, but more that they want to eliminate any reasons for existing customers to switch to competitors because of AI. That being said, CRM is as good of an AI use case as it gets. The field benefits from insights on customers, as well as increasing the speed of the sales pipelines across teams.

At the very least, modeling-in growth rates becomes easier as the ceiling keeps closing. This leads me to my growth estimates for Salesforce up to 10% for the next five years, converging on the risk-free thereafter.

Salesforce Will Engage In Disciplined Acquisitions - But It’s Because Of A Depressed M&A Market

Salesforce recently entered into an $8B equity agreement to acquire Informatica (INFA) – A company that produces software allowing customers to migrate and integrate data from multiple vendors to their preferred platform. Salesforce is betting that Informatica’s data management platform will help structure customer data more effectively - driving better performance from its AI models.

While I view Salesforce’s acquisition approach as a mixed bag, I am ok with buying companies in this environment as rough financial conditions allow the company to pay more reasonable prices. In fact, INFA peaked twice just below $40 per share in the last 5 years, and Salesforce managed to get it at a slight premium of $25 compared to the pre-announcement price around $19 per share.

I don’t necessarily mind more acquisitions per se, but the problem I have is that I’m not seeing viable organic growth opportunities for the company, and competitors are chirping away at the software’s moat.

Growing Along Industry Lines

Part of the reason why I estimate that Salesforce will be able to sustain growth rates around 10% is the support from the general industry growth. I estimate the customer relationship market TAM (1, 2) between $76B and $101B, with a 2024 midpoint at $89B. From this, CRM holds some 43%, and rising peers like HubSpot (HUBS) have about 3.4%.

By applying a discount to the estimates from my sources above, I project that the industry will grow to $210B in 2032, a CAGR of 13%. However, I will keep my growth estimates for Salesforce up to 10% as I expect that most of the projected TAM growth will be allocated to companies like HUBS and MNDY that are targeting the SMB [small to medium business] market. In other words, I expect growth to come from smaller businesses adopting CRM solutions but expect them to look for bargains in software pricing.

Given that Salesforce primarily sells its software to larger corporations, and already has a substantial share of this market, I expect it to become more difficult for them to add customers.

The New CRM Landscape

Salesforce’s competitive landscape has changed from being the only game in town for enterprise-level CRM, to facing competitors from the SMB space as well as large peers seeking to expand software across their customers’ sales cycle.

Considering large peers, Salesforce is facing Microsoft with its Dynamics platform as well as Oracle with their CX and ERP offerings. From below, the company primarily faces peers like HUBS that is developing an SMB CRM + Marketing automation platform, as well as monday.com (MNDY) which has recently entered the CRM space.

In my view, most of these companies started off their value proposition within a specialized niche, however, their product offerings are starting to quickly overlap as they attempt to capture an increasing portion of the sales/business cycle. For example, ORCL built a business around relational databases, and expanded to produce ready-made products built around their database core. Microsoft started their journey on the productivity side with Office, and is now expanding by connecting Office to KPIs via Dynamics. HubSpot tailored their CRM to include SMBs’ pain-points such as marketing automation and is now moving up the stack by targeting enterprise-level customers. MNDY started with their “Work OS” [project management & insights], and has now added a customer service and CRM offering to their stack.

Regardless of whether investors think which of these approaches will capture a larger portion of the market, the base case now is that the landscape is more crowded, which I expect to result in stunted margin growth.

From Salesforce, I expect it to continue being the leading enterprise-level CRM due to its brand name and embeddedness in the industry. While there is disruption happening in the space, I view that it will impact Salesforce’s pricing power - as peers increase supplementary products, but won’t lead to switching in large customers.

How Salesforce Stacks-up To Peers

Salesforce has the lowest forward EV/Sales among peers, but it doesn’t mean it is undervalued. In my view, the company also has the lowest growth prospects, so one may go so far as to say the ratio may further compress.

The table below shows how the companies compare both on the fundamentals and pricing:

I outline my expectations for the other competitors below:

  • Despite being the primary contender, HUBS customers have limited budgets, and betting on it implies an expectation of SMB economic recovery as well as some degree of upwards penetration to enterprise-level customers.

  • MNDY is entering the industry via the project/operations management side. The company is having success penetrating enterprise-level customers but not necessarily cross-selling the CRM software to them [yet]. In their latest quarterly report, the number of customers that produce more than $100K ARR [larger companies] increased by 46% to 1328. Success in this space will open a further argument for customer procurement teams to negotiate down Salesforce margins.

  • While Microsoft nailed the market with Teams they can’t include dynamics as a cross-sell to their Office product suite. This is why they are selling it as a standalone product, but have lost the first-mover advantage in the space and their software comes with the enterprise bloat pushing it down lower in management-decision rankings. AI is promising to be a disruptor, and may take market share from Salesforce. However, the way I see this playing out is by including AI-powered CRM features to the Office suite, instead of increasing the value proposition of Dynamics with AI.

  • Oracle has carved a niche in the database world and will keep building products that cater to customers that need their software [CRM and ERP] to work with a huge amount of records. While the company’s tech is a serious competitor, user experience reports aren’t the best when it comes to working with ORCL’s software, contributing to slower SaaS growth.

An unexpected M&A can seriously change the game for all competitors, which is why these companies should be monitored together. Investors that are looking for exposure in the CRM space can go beta and consider bundling up Salesforce, HUBS, and MSFT. While if you are “seeking alpha”, you may want to consider underdogs like HUBS or MNDY – both high growth & risk premium stocks but sufficiently divergent in the market they are targeting.

The key factor governing the pricing multiple starts with margins, specifically potential future margins – discussed below.

Hidden Costs And Adjusted Operating Margins

As noted above, the difference between the right multiple for Salesforce being closer to 5x or 10x EV/Sales is the expectation of future margins. If we start with the story that Salesforce management wants to anchor investors to, it would be that over time the company should converge and possibly surpass the adjusted operating margins of 34%.

There are two rough ways we can look at adj. margins:

  1. The adjustment is representative of the true margin, and the company will eventually converge the GAAP to the non-GAAP margin. Typically, this takes the form of outgrowing fixed costs, and reducing the investments needed in the building-phase of a product by switching to maintenance expenses and a profit-taking mode of operations.

  2. The GAAP measure is representative of reality, and the non-GAAP measure contains hidden costs that can’t be easily outgrown. This assumes that most costs are variable, and a function of the number of customers the company is serving. While software is great at scaling gross margins, it is less so in scaling account [CRM] management, and companies find themselves doing more for customers instead of making it cheaper to service accounts. The same goes for marketing – as it gets cheaper, managers keep allocating more to defend against competitors in bad times and push for growth in good times.

One way to check which of these is closer to the truth is to compare them with software industry averages. Salesforce’s adj. op. margin guide of 34% is somewhat off from the industry average of 29%.

In my view, the factors that can increase the operating margin in the future are a reduction in marketing expenses. This is because Salesforce has a somewhat sticky product, where most of the expenses lie in customer acquisition. Once a customer is added, it is a non-trivial project for them to switch to a competitive product – this is especially valid for larger companies that are the primary value driver for Salesforce.

This is also connected to the argument that Salesforce’s SBC is a growth driver, as the mentioned expenses are associated with the front-loaded costs for customer acquisition. However, I view that this aspect of SBC is already optimized and most of the SBC is the variable portion that is going to persist as a fraction of revenues.

For these reasons, I am willing to increase the operating margin target over time to account for a reduction of future customer acquisition expenses, but I am not willing to go so far as to write off SBC as a declining expense.

As a result of the mentioned competitive risk factors and possible disruption, I am settling for a base operating margin target of 30% by 2030.

How Salesforce Can Defend Against Disruptors

In my view, one of the ways that Salesforce can go on the offensive against peers like MNDY & MSFT is to acquire Asana (ASAN).

As noted in my previous analysis, ASAN has a great product, and a customer list of large companies. However, it’s sticking to its core product and hasn’t created offerings across the sales chain, resulting in it being pressured by competitors. Because of this, they may be a good fit in an established ecosystem, allowing them to fulfill their enterprise-target mission, cut costs on developing in-house AI, and increase the value proposition of Salesforce as a bundle.

The stock seems to be significantly beaten down from the highs [priced around $4.3B], allowing for negotiating leverage in an acquisition.

Valuation Model

Putting all of the parts together, we gain the intrinsic value model for Salesforce. Here is a summary of the assumptions:

  • Growth up to 10% in the next 5 years, converging on the risk-free rate after that. Investors assuming sustained growth for longer, should try extending the forecast period instead of going with larger terminal growth.

  • Operating margin converges to 30% in year 5 as the base case.

  • I expect Salesforce to keep struggling with expensive M&A. Net of depreciation [>$3B yearly], I project around an annualized $3B total reinvestment [CapEx + M&A] after the Informatica acquisition.

  • While a risk-free rate of 4.5% plus an ERP of 4% is on the low side for a company like this, the way I see the current market environment is that investors are taking what they can get, not necessarily the full price of risk. Additionally, Salesforce is entering a relatively stable phase of operations, which is why I am content with a lower required return.

At the end of the forecast period, I estimate that Salesforce will be able to produce revenues of around $80B, and operating profit of $24B at a 30% margin.

The resulting unlevered free cash flows start around $5.2B for 2026, and rise between $17B and $19B in the final years of my forecast period. At this point, if investors disagree that Salesforce can produce such high free cash flows, they should adjust accordingly.

Conclusion

I believe that despite the tense competitive environment and technological turnover, Salesforce will be able to switch to a profitability mode and start delivering significantly more free cash flows to investors.

The resulting intrinsic value for Salesforce is around $196B or $204 per share. Over my forecast period, I estimate that Salesforce will grow its free cash flows incrementally by 13%, which results in my 1-year price target of $230 per share. The 13% FCFF growth is in excess of my 8.5% required return, which is why I see Salesforce as a value generating company, whose current price of $263 is some 2 years ahead of the intrinsic value. This makes it a premium stock, but one whose fundamentals can converge to its intrinsic value in a relatively short period.

Conversely, the implied forward pricing is an EV/Revenue of 5.3x.

I rate the company a “Buy”, and expect it to keep increasing in excess value as long as it keeps adding to the [GAAP] operating margin, which is my KPI for the stock’s performance.

Risks And Considerations

  • I expect the market to curtail its enthusiasm towards the stock as it waits to see what will be the result of the disruption threats presented by MSFT and smaller peers. Should Salesforce’s Agentforce AI work protectively, I expect the market to re-price the company back to a 5-year premium, else the stock will keep converging down to its intrinsic value. Note that the intrinsic value is not static, and can rise more than 9% per year due to growth and the resulting excess returns.

  • The Salesforce analysis is part of my CRM/Project management software collection where I analyzed key peers in this space independently, and is partly where my conclusions are coming from. The notes for the companies I covered in this bundle are: HUBS, MNDY, ASAN.

  • Despite high switching costs, high-value customers may be willing to add and later switch to another CRM vendor should the value proposition become compelling. Enterprise-level software is wide [seeks to cover surface features] and can be difficult to work with, but some of the smaller competitors may produce more efficient software, enticing customers to switch.

  • A much more pressing risk factor is the amount of innovation in this space due to the general advancement in web-development, as well as AI. This will embolden customers to increasingly negotiate annual contract pricing and pressure Salesforce’s margins.

  • While tailored CRMs never really took off [e.g. The Sage Group (OTCPK:SGPYY)], the technology may have been early, and we can see a resurgence in specialized software for particular industries.

data.title: Heineken: Growth Has Come At The Expense Of Profitability
data.description:
Headquartered in the Netherlands, Heineken N.V. (OTCQX:HEINY) produces and sells beer, cider, seltzer, wine, energy drinks, other beverages, and spirits globally. The company has a diversified geographic footprint; in 2024, 38.7% of Heineken’s revenues came from Europe, 34.0% from the Americas, 13.8% from the Asia Pacific region, and 13.5% from Africa and the Middle East.

While the stock has previously appreciated at a good pace, Heineken’s stock has performed much worse in the past few years. The beer category has shown weak growth, pressuring brewers across the world in especially developed markets. With younger people drinking less, a weak industry-wide growth trend is set to continue. Heineken pays out a dividend with a current yield of 2.3%.

Heineken Has Achieved Good Growth at the Expense of Profitability

Heineken’s long-term growth is quite good. The company has grown revenues at a 2002-2024 CAGR of 5.9% in a most often stable manner, reaching a total of €29.8 billion in 2024. Heineken has expanded past its historically dominant position in Europe, achieving especially good growth in the Americas but also in other regions during the past decade. Heineken remains one of the largest brewers in Europe alongside Anheuser-Busch InBev SA/NV (BUD) and others.

The long-term growth has been accompanied by fair, but not very accretive profitability. Heineken has consistently achieved double-digit operating margins with a 2002-2024 average of 13.2%. The margin level trails ABInBev’s current 25.8% operating margin and Constellation Brands, Inc.'s (STZ) 34.4% operating margin among others quite significantly – brewers most often have great margins, but Heineken hasn’t achieved such high margins due to significantly lower gross margins. The lower margin level has carried onto returns on investments as well, as Heineken’s return on total capital is at just 6.0%, nearly exactly at my estimate for the company’s cost of capital. The similar return on capital and cost of capital makes Heineken’s growth not very valuable for shareholders.

Ultimately, it appears that Heineken has leveraged a value-oriented pricing strategy to capture more aggressive volume growth over the company’s history. Given the relatively modest return on investment, I believe that the strategy isn’t too shareholder-friendly for now. Heineken’s efforts in portfolio premiumization, investments in alcohol-free beer brands, and other initiatives need to create better pricing power before Heineken’s growth is a highly accretive use of capital.

Slow Beer Category Growth Is Now Challenging Heineken’s Growth Outlook

Zooming into Heineken’s more recent performance, the company has struggled due to slower beer category growth. On top of higher consumer uncertainty, young people are choosing to drink less alcohol, causing weak industry projections in especially most developed markets. Beer demand is expected to show a very slow decline in Europe and in the U.S., two of Heineken’s critical markets, while many emerging markets are still projected to show somewhat better volume growth. Heineken’s addition of alcohol-free alternatives has somewhat aided in mitigating the impact, but the trend is a clear negative for Heineken regardless.

Heineken’s recent financials reflect such weak trends. Total revenues turned to a slight -1.8% decline in 2024, and while organic growth was still slightly positive, volumes only showed quite moderate 1.4% organic growth. Further, to start 2025, the first quarter’s sales performance turned volume growth negative with a -2.1% organic decline in beer volume, led by a -4.7% decline in Europe and a -3.7% decline in the Americas. While some decline was related to factors such as Easter’s timing and macroeconomic turbulence, the weak trend in developing markets seems to also reflect the slow secular decline in demand for alcohol. Total revenues declined by -4.9% in the quarter. Heineken still notes a subdued European consumer sentiment, volatility in emerging markets, and geopolitical fluctuation in the 2025 outlook.

I have previously also noted Molson Coors Beverage Company’s (TAP) weak performance in this article. ABInBev’s performance has also been weak in the U.S., albeit the company’s sales outlook is improving, as I wrote in a recent article.

Although Heineken’s sales outlook is quite weak, the company’s earnings outlook remains resilient for 2025. Heineken anticipates at least €400 million in gross savings during the year to offset weaker revenues, and as a result, guides for healthy 4-8% organic growth in adjusted operating income. Yet, over the longer term, the more concerning industry trends pose a threat to Heineken’s historical earnings growth rate as a temporary cost savings program seems to be the only factor driving Heineken’s 2025 resilience.

Heineken’s Valuation Is Fair

I built a discounted cash flow (DCF) model for Heineken’s stock. While I expect the current revenue weakness from macroeconomic turbulence to subside after 2025, I expect the weakening secular demand for alcohol to pressure Heineken’s revenue growth below the long-term rate. I estimate revenue growth to stabilize at a 2% perpetual level from 2027 forward.

I expect Heineken’s margin level to stay the same from 2025 forward; the ongoing cost savings program will offset earnings pressure from lower short-term volumes, and afterwards, I assume Heineken’s pricing power to stay similar. With the estimated growth, Heineken shouldn’t need too significant investments, making the after-tax cash flow conversion quite good.

The estimates put Heineken’s fair value at $45.73, very near the stock price at the time of writing – below-historical growth is already priced in. For a good buying opportunity, I believe that Heineken would need to prove improved pricing power, or the stock would need to decline noticeably.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital [WACC] of 6.08%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I estimate a 4.30% interest rate, annualized from H2 interest expenses and debt. I expect Heineken to continue leveraging a good amount of debt with a 25% long-term debt-to-equity ratio.

For cost of equity, I use the 10-year EUR swap yield of 2.58% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.33% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent January estimates for the Netherlands. Seeking Alpha estimates Heineken’s beta at 0.58. With an ESG add-on of 1.5% and a liquidity premium of 0.2%, the cost of equity stands at 6.79% and the WACC at 6.08%.

Takeaway

Heineken N.V.’s long-term growth has been good, but it has come at the expense of profitability as the company’s return on capital isn’t too accretive. Weakening demand for alcohol in many developed markets is challenging Heineken’s growth on top of macroeconomic turbulence, turning beer volume growth negative in Q1. I expect the secular decline to continue pressuring Heineken’s growth below the long-term historical rate. Heineken’s stock is valued fairly, and as such, I initiate the stock at a Hold rating.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Humana: Implied Volatility Creating A Viable Investment Case
data.description:
In our previous coverage of Humana Inc. (NYSE:HUM), we urged investors to be cautious as the earnings estimates were far from reality.

So what does this mean? It means that those two numbers down there are likely to be completely wrong.

We are going out on a limb here and saying even 2025 will be completely far away from estimates as HUM works hard to get the star ratings up. We will have to wait to do more modeling on this after the full impact is clarified (likely during the Q3-2024 conference call), but we expect 2025 earnings to be flat ($14.50-$17 per share) relative to 2024. We would expect a big drop in 2026 and this is what the recent selloff is about. Our guesstimate is that HUM will struggle to clear $12.00 of earnings per share.

Source: 2026 Earnings Likely To Be Crushed

That was the thesis and this has been the result.

Let's look at how the numbers are shaping and see if we can find a way to play this.

2025 Estimates

Analysts have finally gotten onboard with the headwinds and have downgraded the estimates at a rapid clip since our last work. At $16.35, the EPS estimates are actually now within the band we suggested they would fall within.

Estimates are beginning to stabilize, and we can see there were actually a few revisions higher as well. The stock has digested most of that bad news and the bears have not made much progress despite that.

2026 Estimates

This was where we expected the most meat on the bone for the bears. The EPS estimates were at $25.53 at the time. $14.50 seems far more reasonable though we see more downside risk here all the way to $12.00 per share.

With both numbers though closer to their potential troughs than they were 8 months back, can we make the bull case?

Outlook

Health insurers have always been a very low-margin business. If you compare what they make to pharmaceuticals and biotechnology companies, you will understand what we are talking about. You can see below where Pfizer Inc. (PFE), Merck & Co., Inc. (MRK) and Gilead Sciences, Inc. (GILD) stand relative to UnitedHealth Group Incorporated (UNH) and Centene Corporation (CNC).

Our protagonist, Humana, is missing here because, for some reason, YCharts was not ready to pull up this data. However, we can verify that the company averages gross margins generally close to 15%. While those are smaller numbers than most healthcare companies, it still results in a lot of government money, especially when you have such a huge Medicaid concentration.

The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services made some major announcements that were aimed at reducing potential overpayments to private insurers and even claw back some amounts made in previous years.

The Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services has introduced sweeping reforms aimed at curbing anti-competitive practices, enhancing transparency, and promoting equity. Key changes impacting Humana include:

  1. Star Ratings Controversy: CMS revised its methodology for calculating MA Star Ratings in 2025, drastically lowering Humana's scores. For instance, its flagship H5216 plan plummeted from 4.5 to 3.5 stars, leaving only 25% of its membership in 4-star or higher plans—a stark contrast to 94% in 2024. This downgrade has severe financial consequences, as quality bonus payments now evaporate.

  2. Agent Compensation Overhaul: CMS capped agent commissions at $411 per enrollment to prevent steering toward high-commission plans. While this limits unfair incentives, it may reduce sales force motivation, exacerbating Humana's membership decline.

  3. Data Privacy and Marketing Restrictions: Prohibitions on sharing beneficiary data without explicit consent and stricter oversight of TPMO contracts aim to curb aggressive marketing.

Source: AInvest

The new measures aim to claw back about $9 billion in estimated Medicaid payments (this amount applies to Humana alone) made between 2018 and 2024. This number was based on an extrapolation of the 2023 numbers, though we doubt anyone has any certainty as to their validity. The hits don't stop for the company just there. The current bill in the Senate designed to cut taxes for the upper brackets also comes with spending cuts for Medicaid. While the timelines are uncertain and the bill still faces a ton of opposition, even from Republicans, this creates more trauma to the growth story. The growth story we are referring to is the fairy tale that Humana's investors will live happily ever after as the earnings go vertical in the back half of this decade.

Based on where things stand, we see three reasons why that will just not happen. The first being that the lower the actual earnings at the front end (2026-2027) the lower the earnings at the back end. This is simply from a buyback perspective as the less shares Humana retires, the less the EPS will rise during 2028-2029, all other things being equal. The second reason is that there will be at least some clawback and that will require Humana to go into balance sheet repair mode. The rating agencies are watching carefully and there are good reasons why Fitch has their outlook of "Negative".

The Rating Outlook on HUM's 'BBB+' Long-Term Issuer Default Rating was revised to Negative from Stable on May 3, 2024. HUM maintains adequate capitalization and leverage, which is a moderately weighted factor in its ratings. While HUM's financial leverage ratio was generally within Fitch's expectations of 40% or below at YE 2024, margin pressure due to elevated utilization in the senior population in 2024 resulted in a debt/EBITDA ratio near 4.6x for the year, which is significantly above Fitch's expectations for the company's current ratings.

Fitch will continue to monitor HUM's operating performance as 2025 unfolds to determine whether initiatives implemented by management, including exits from Medicare Advantage plans with high benefit ratios and benefit adjustments to remaining plans, will be sufficient to materially improve earnings-based financial leverage measures within a timeframe consistent with Fitch's typical rating horizon. Fitch will also continue to monitor developments around litigation filed by HUM in October 2024 against certain government agencies related to the company's 2025 Star Ratings.

Source: Fitch.

So no buybacks and all those back-end estimates continue to incorporate a lot of that.

Finally, we see Humana's debt rollover posing a material threat with higher rates. The combination are more bloated balance sheet with potentially lower credit ratings means that you could be looking at sub $20 per share earnings as far as the eye can see.

Verdict

When we first wrote about Humana in 2024, we suggested investors holster their bullish views as Humana was not as cheap as it looked.

The rationale then was that while it looked "cheap" from a P/E standpoint, price-to-sales multiples told a different story. Margin reversion would be nasty for Humana's model and that has been the story for the last 18 months. The good part is that we are approaching the troughs of 2008 and 2000 on this multiple.

The bad part is that an exact match would create another 50% downside. So the risk remains incredibly high that we could see mediocre returns from here in the very best scenarios. The good part here for investors is that options are actually offering a good risk-reward here, and you just have to get the thesis half-right to make money. Here we a referring to longer-dated options and not the monthly ones that buy-write funds sell blindly. The $230 calls offer over 16% annualized returns and all that needs to happen is that Humana needs to do modestly well and stabilize its operations.

On the downside, your breakeven is $179.00, which, we think, is a good price to actually get long Humana. More aggressive investors could consider selling 2 calls at this strike for every 100 shares. That would mean that would actually go short 100 shares at a net price of $323 if Humana holds over $230 by January 2027. But this strategy would also reduce their cost basis down to $131 if Humana holds under $230 18 months from now.

Please note that this is not financial advice. It may seem like it, sound like it, but surprisingly, it is not. Investors are expected to do their own due diligence and consult a professional who knows their objectives and constraints.

data.title: Silver Is Breaking Out - Here's What You Need To Know (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
By Jesse Colombo

Silver futures have broken out, delivering a strong bullish signal—but I’m still looking for additional confirmation to strengthen the case.

I’ve been consistently bullish on silver and have been pointing out the key signals to watch for a breakout from its prolonged consolidation of the past year. I’ve repeatedly said the main trigger would be a decisive, high-volume move above two critical resistance zones: $32 to $33 and then $34 to $35.

While many grew weary of waiting and doubted it would happen, it finally did yesterday—which is incredibly exciting. That’s why I’m publishing this quick technical update to show you where silver stands now and what I’m looking for next to confirm that the bull market is fully underway.

The primary form of silver I monitor is COMEX silver futures, as they tend to respect $1 increments as key support and resistance levels and, unlike the spot price, also display trading volume—an essential indicator of conviction and institutional money flows.

Remarkably, silver finally broke through the critical $35 threshold yesterday on strong volume, delivering a clear and decisive bullish signal. This signal remains valid as long as silver holds above that level. I believe this breakout sets the stage for a rapid move to $40, $50, $60, and beyond, as outlined in my detailed report linked in the first paragraph of this update.

One of the immediate catalysts behind yesterday's breakout is China’s rare earth metals export curbs. However, the reality is that this breakout was inevitable—and it is primarily technical in nature. Silver has been like a coiled spring, simply waiting for a spark to unleash its momentum, and that’s exactly what we’re seeing now.

In addition, precious metals have been gaining support from a combination of recent developments: Moody’s downgrade of U.S. debt, continued weakness in the U.S. dollar, escalating tariff tensions between the U.S. and China, the Trump administration’s controversial new budget, and rising political dysfunction—fueled further by Elon Musk’s public falling-out with President Trump.

As I’ve noted before, silver’s frustrating consolidation over the past year has reminded me of gold’s prolonged range from 2020 to 2024—a period I said would eventually give way to a major bull market once gold broke above its $2,000 to $2,100 resistance zone, which it ultimately did.

I believe silver is now following a similar path, and yesterday's breakout has a very strong chance of marking the beginning of a powerful new bull market.

I’ve also developed a proprietary tool called the Synthetic Silver Price Index (SSPI), designed to help validate silver’s price action and filter out potential false breakouts.

The SSPI is calculated as the average of gold and copper prices, with copper scaled by a factor of 540 to prevent gold from dominating the index. Interestingly, even though silver isn’t part of the calculation, the SSPI closely tracks its movements.

I’ve been highlighting how the SSPI has been trading within a consolidation range between 2,800 and 3,000. A breakout above the 3,000 level would serve as a strong bullish confirmation for silver.

Yesterday, the SSPI initially rallied—driven by a sharp upward move in copper—but as copper gave back much of its gains, the SSPI failed to close above the key 3,000 level. Despite that, momentum remains strong, and I believe there’s a high probability the SSPI will make another breakout attempt soon. This, along with a decisive breakout in euro-priced silver, is one of the final confirmations I’m watching for.

The copper futures chart below shows how prices tested—but failed to break above—the key $5.00 to $5.20 resistance zone, which held back both the SSPI and silver from achieving even further gains yesterday. For more on my outlook for copper and why I’m bullish on it alongside silver, read my recent report.

In addition to silver itself, I’m also highly bullish on silver mining stocks and ETFs, which I expect to deliver even larger gains in the coming bull market due to their leverage to the price of silver. Large silver miners, as tracked by the SIL ETF, have been performing strongly after breaking out of a long-term triangle pattern that dates all the way back to 2011.

SIL is now approaching a critical resistance zone between $48 and $52—a level that has repeatedly capped rallies since 2016. I believe a decisive breakout above this zone, especially in tandem with a confirmed silver bull market, will trigger an explosive move higher in silver mining stocks. I’m watching closely and very excited about the potential upside.

Silver junior mining stocks, as tracked by the SILJ ETF, are even more volatile than their larger-cap counterparts—and offer even greater profit potential during bull markets.

Currently, SILJ remains within a long-term triangle pattern that dates back to 2013. I believe that once it breaks out of this formation, both SILJ and the broader junior silver mining space are poised to surge dramatically—offering significant upside for those positioned ahead of the move.

While not an absolute necessity, one final confirmation of the silver bull market would be a decisive breakdown in the U.S. dollar, which historically trades inversely to commodities—including precious metals.

As I’ve explained recently, the key level to watch on the U.S. Dollar Index (DXY) is 100. The index is now trading below that threshold, giving it a clear bearish bias. The next critical level is 98; if that support breaks, I believe the index will quickly move toward the next major support at 90. Such a move would act as rocket fuel for the bull market in gold, silver, and mining stocks.

To summarize, yesterday's nascent breakout in silver is both encouraging and exciting. However, I’m looking for additional confirmation before calling it a full-scale breakout. Specifically, I want to see silver break out in euros, the Synthetic Silver Price Index (SSPI) move decisively higher, and the U.S. Dollar Index break down further.

Once those pieces fall into place, I truly believe the major silver bull market I’ve been calling for—and that many of us have been waiting on—will finally be underway. As always, I’ll continue to keep you updated.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: May Jobs Report Surprises, Despite Mixed JOLTS And ADP
data.description:
Introduction

The May Jobs Report released by the US BLS on June 6th was reported as "a healthy gain," with the report showing an add of 139K jobs, higher than the 126K consensus estimate.

The overall picture is still looking relatively good from this perspective, especially with the unemployment rate (both U-3 and U-6) staying at the same level month-to-month. The current level of unemployment is considered "natural," or the level at which we should expect in a functioning economy, as it is normal to have some level of churn in labor markets.

Area most improved in the report:

  • Healthcare +62,000 jobs
  • Leisure & Hospitality +48,000 jobs

Area hit the worst in the report:

  • Manufacturing -8,000 jobs
  • Federal employment -22,000 jobs

Although, it should be of note that the loss in federal jobs was offset by state and government jobs, which added 21,000. This leads to the total "government" category to a loss of 1,000 jobs, not necessarily the headline "22,000" that I saw reported alongside the release of this data.

Just like last time, the BLS did disappoint with heavy revisions to past jobs numbers:

The change in total nonfarm payroll employment for March was revised down by 65,000, from +185,000 to +120,000, and the change for April was revised down by 30,000, from +177,000 to +147,000. With these revisions, employment in March and April combined is 95,000 lower than previously reported.

A largely negative highlight on the report: temporary agency positions fell by 20,000. This is a particularly negative sign because firms are usually less likely to cut temporary hiring over permanent hiring. This could be a sign of early softening, but it's still too soon to tell. It could also be noise and the trend could correct in the coming months.

Impact of the May Jobs Report

The job’s report was ultimately good, but only in its own context, as rate changes from the Fed are likely to be decided on by inflation levels, with the labor market acting as a canary.

I see this report as dispelling some discord brought on by other jobs data this week, that has caused markets to send mixed signals. Just this past week, there were two other jobs reports that are worth taking a look at, to see how they diverge from the BLS figures that are normally the largest headline, and move markets the most.

Following the BLS report on June 6th, the US major indices rallied nearly 1% across the board:

It's important for us to look at that other data and take a look at the reason there was a lower expectation for this BLS report than what we got. The rest of the data this past week wasn't as pretty.

Other Jobs Data Has Been Divergent

There's been a notable divergence in the jobs reports from this past week, looking at both the JOLTS and ADP reports, released within days of this latest jobs report from the BLS. The data tells us different parts of the overall story of the economy, and highlight the differences in data collection and survey participation between sectors in the labor market. It's important to get all sides of a story when it comes to the economy, as we can often miss the truth by focusing in on one data point too deeply.

JOLTS Data

Here is the JOLTS data from this week, where its April hires figures blew away the rest of the year for hires, indicating a strong demand for labor, and the strongest month YTD.

Figures are measured in thousands.

That dataset also showed us that layoffs were on the rise as well, and has risen again to similar levels to back in February, despite a dip in the March figures released last month.

Openings are down to the third-lowest point in a while, indicating how tight the market is getting as layoffs and discharges rise.

We can also see this "tightness" in the quits figure, as fewer and fewer people are quitting their jobs from YTD figures, more in line with the end of last year, and lower than the 2024 average.

When we combine all the separations into one category, we get a figure that is nearly in line with the hiring figure, showing us that "churn" that we see in the BLS report as well, but a "churn" that is fairly stagnant.

ADP Employment Data

The worst report of the week was by far the ADP survey, where private payrolls sat at a 2-year low, down to 37K.

Just like in the BLS report (corroboration is a good thing between these reports), leisure and hospitality were one of the strongest sectors.

Information jobs, largely tech roles, have been on the rise after a sharp downturn in April, and remain mixed overall.

However, and this was particularly negative on the ADP report, we saw a very low figure for construction in May; not as low as March, but this makes March less of an outlier and more of an indicator for the new trend.

Manufacturing also disappointed on the report, with a loss of 3k jobs. This does come after a large correction and massive swings in job counts in the sector this year.

Conclusion

The May Jobs Report from the BLS confirms and denies some suppositions the market has been making about the labor market, given the mixed data we saw in the JOLTS and ADP reports this week. Ultimately, the BLS report casts a brighter light on the economy than the other reports. We have seen a few patterns emerge that I will be following:

  • Services are outperforming goods, e.g., leisure over manufacturing.
  • Layoffs increasing across the board, showing potential weakness.
  • Data divergence between job postings and hiring is telling of business sentiment.
    • They seem more eager to post jobs than to fill them, showing weakness.
  • State governments are absorbing many federal job losses, meaning the headline federal job losses are potentially not true government losses on the whole, just a movement from federal to state level positions.

I will be looking forward to the next Fed meeting on June 18th to see what comments the FOMC makes about the labor market. I expect them to be hawkish at this next meeting, and hold rates steady. This report won't be enough for them to cut, as the mixed signals don't show enough of a downtrend for them to worry just yet.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: Autolus: A Buy Before Key Data Readout, Potential Expansion Into Autoimmune Market
data.description:
Investment Overview

When I last covered Autolus Therapeutics plc (NASDAQ:AUTL) for Seeking Alpha back in September last year, it was to make a Bull case for its shares, based on the UK-based biotech's upcoming PDUFA date for its autologous CAR-T cell therapy, obecabtagene autoleucel ("obe-cel"), in patients with relapsed/refractory (r/r) adult B-cell Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia (B-ALL).

Unfortunately, in 2024 we witnessed the emergence of a worrying new trend of drug approvals creating more negative market sentiment than positive, and although Autolus reported on November 8th, 2024, that obe-cel had secured approval, the brand name AUCATZYL, after the briefest of spikes from ~$3.3 per share, to $4, shares began to lose value, sinking as low as $1.15 per share in April this year.

Quoting from Autolus' press release, here is how the company summarizes the data, from its pivotal FELIX study, that led to U.S. approval:

In the efficacy evaluable patients (n=65), 63% achieved overall complete remission (OCR1) which includes 51% of patients with CR at any time and 12% of patients with CRi at any time. The major efficacy outcome was complete remission within 3 months, which was achieved in 42% of patients, and the median duration of remission (DOR) was 14.1 months.

AUCATZYL showed low levels of Cytokine Release Syndrome (CRS), with 3% Grade 3 events, and no Grade 4 or 5 events. Grade ≥ 3 Immune Effector Cell-Associated Neurotoxicity Syndrome (ICANS) was reported in 7% of patients. No REMS was required by the FDA for AUCATZYL.

At the end of last month, Autolus announced that AUCATZYL has been recommended for EU approval by the Committee for Medicinal Products for Human Use ("CHMP"), based on data from Felix discussed below:

In the pivotal cohort of patients, (cohort IIA (n=94)), the Complete Response/Complete Response with Incomplete Haematological Recovery (CR/CRi) for patients who received at least one infusion of obecabtagene autoleucel was 76.6%. The median response duration for all infused patients was 21.2 months. Median event-free survival (EFS) was 11.9 months and the estimated 6- and 12-month event-free survival rates were 65.4% and 49.5%, respectively.

The most common non-laboratory Grade 3 or higher adverse reactions were infections-pathogen unspecified (32%), febrile neutropenia (24%), and bacterial infectious disorders (11%). Cytokine release syndrome developed in 87 of the 127 patients (68.5%), with events of grade 3 or higher in three patients (2.4%). Immune effector cell-associated neurotoxicity syndrome developed in 29 of the 127 patients (22.8%), with grade 3 or higher occurring in nine patients (7%).

The reason I am sharing summaries of both datasets is that they seemingly establish AUCATZYL as the best-in-class treatment in its field. Gilead Sciences, Inc. (GILD) Tecartus was approved to treat B-ALL in 2021, earning revenues of >$400m last year, based on a 65% complete remission and 13.6 months DoR. AUCATZYL's two data sets average out at a slightly superior CR rate and superior median DoR.

In terms of safety, Tecartus's study resulted in a 26% and 35% percentage of patients experiencing Grade 3 or higher adverse events related to cytokine release syndrome ("CRS") and neurologic events respectively. Seemingly, AUCATZYL's study has a much lower rate of Grade 3+ CRS at ~3%. Kymriah, also marketed and sold by Gilead Sciences, Inc. (GILD) is also approved for ALL, but only "for certain pediatric and young adult patients."

Autolus has apparently priced AUCATZYL higher than tecartus, at $525k, and analysts have speculated it could earn peak revenues of ~$300m per annum.

Autolus has invested heavily in developing AUCATZYL, and in a 70,000-square foot facility called the Nucleus, in Stevenage, United Kingdom, which the company says (in its 2024 annual report/10-K submission):

has a global reach, and can meet our near and mid-term clinical and commercial needs allowing ample time for expanding our manufacturing footprint...at full capacity, we expect the Nucleus facility to provide manufacturing capacity for approximately 2,000 batches annually.

Autolus has made losses of $(221m), $(208m), $(149m), and $(142m) across the past four years, working backward, hence, given the peak sales potential of its only commercial asset, perhaps its current share price of $2.3, and market cap valuation of $560m is warranted.

Nevertheless, in this note I plan to look a little deeper into the strengths, weaknesses, opportunities, and threats associated with this company, to see if buying stock today or continuing to hold stock if you already own it, is the right strategy.

Autolus - Q1 2025 In Review AUCATZYL's Strong Launch

For Q1 2025, Autolus reported AUCATZYL revenues of $9m, which was ahead of analysts' expectations, although the net loss of $(70.2m), or $(0.26) per share, was wider than expected. Autolus reported a cash position of $96m, with $421m of near-term cash, so even at the current burn rate, it would not be exhausted until 2027, we can speculate.

Autolus says it has reimbursement in place from commercial and government patients, and 39 treatment centers authorized to deliver AUCATZYL. The company's ambition is to have 60 centers online, which it says, "will give us approximately 90% access to patients across the U.S."

Nevertheless, it's clear that in order to succeed and grow as a business, Autolus needs to turn losses into profits, and one obvious way to do that is to earn more revenues.

Autolus has not provided any revenue - or expenses - guidance for 2025, so far as I am aware. Interestingly, analysts on the Q1 earnings call noted that Tecartus revenues in Q1 were ~$78m, down from $100m in the prior year, and asked if this impact could have been related to the launch of AUCATZYL?

Autolus' CEO Christian Itin speculated it would more likely be related to the mantle cell lymphoma market which tecartus is also approved in, where it faces increasing competition from Bristol-Myers Squibb Company's (BMY) Breyanzi. When asked what percentage of Tecartus' Q1 earnings would have come from which indication, Itin suggested it may have been 20-40% of ALL patients two years ago, but it was hard to know given Gilead does not share that data.

This is a long-winded way of suggesting that in its current market, AUCATZYL may do well to earn much more than ~$50m per quarter at its peak, even if it successfully takes market share away from Tecartus, but fortunately, Autolus has advanced plans to expand the number of indications the drug is approved for.

Autolus - Development Plans For AUCATZYL

The most obvious next target for Autolus and AUCATZYL is first-line ALL. As per CEO ITIN, again speaking on the Q1 earnings call.

What we do know from obe-cel and AUCATZYL is that the product when used in patients that are in earlier – in the earlier stages of relapsed or also patients that have lower tumor burden at the time of therapy, the patients do better with those profiles. We believe one of the key areas that I think is important to explore is the utility of the product ultimately in a frontline consolidation setting. We will start to explore – see the exploration of frontline settings through investigator-sponsored trials, and we’ll certainly consider our steps in addition to those.

In fact, Autolus may be able to target other B-cell malignancies - provided its strong efficacy and safety profile holds up in indications such as non-Hodgkin's Lymphoma ("NHL"), or diffuse large B-cell lymphoma ("DLBCL"), AUCATZYL may find itself able to challenge e.g., Gilead's Yescarta, another cell therapy which earned nearly $1.5bn in revenues in 2024.

Intriguingly, Autolus is touting a new long-term data readout from the FELIX study which will presented "mid-year" the company says, and which could help trigger a push into new liquid cancer indications. Pediatric ALL is another indication in which Autolus may have collected enough data to suggest an eventual approval is more likely than not.

As we can see above, besides oncology, Autolus believes AUCATZYL may have a shot at treating autoimmune conditions as well. Phase 1 data from a study in systemic lupus erythematosus ("SLE"), is expected in Q4, while three new Phase 1 studies are expected to initiate, in lupus nephritis, multiple sclerosis ("MS"), and AL amyloidosis.

This isn't unusual for a cell therapy company. Cabaletta Bio, Inc. (CABA), for example, pivoted into autoimmune, citing data from landmark studies conducted by Dr Georg Schett - who was also cited by Autolus' CEO on the Q1 earnings call - in which 8 out of 8 patients with SLE achieved Definition of Remission in SLE (DORIS) remission after a median follow-up of 15 months, after being treated with a cell therapy.

Cabaletta's progress has been slow, and its share price has suffered, so we may conclude that Autolus faces a real challenge in achieving success in this market, although it has shared data from its Phase 1 study in SLE, shown below:

CEO Itin stressed on the earnings call that patients in this study had more advanced disease than those treated in Dr Schett's study, but noted that, after only three months "very quick improvement" in inflammatory processes was observed, and that "three of the six patients did actually achieve renal complete remissions."

The CEO outlined plans for lupus and MS, noting that "obe-cel actually works very well across the blood-brain barrier," which is important in the treatment of MS. Autoimmune markets are vast, running into tens of billions of dollars, and therefore, if Autolus is successful with these studies, it would be reasonable to expect its valuation and share price to climb.

Concluding Thoughts - Autolus Retains My Faith Thanks To Strength Of Key Product

AUCATZYL is an ex-vivo cell therapy, and all such therapies take their toll on patients, who must undergo a form of chemotherapy in order for their cells to be extracted, and reinfused, which requires hospitalization.

On the plus side, AUCATZYL has demonstrated its ability to achieve durable complete remissions in patients and its treatment regime may be the most palatable among currently approved cell therapies. Autolus points out that AUCATZYL is the first CAR-T therapy to have been approved by the FDA with no Risk Evaluation and Mitigation Strategy ("REMS") program.

In order to be successful, I believe Autolus will need to secure further approvals for AUCATZYL, and we should note that, if obe-cel struggles to make the grade in other indications, there are no other drug candidates to fall back on, so there is a high level of "single asset" risk here.

Nevertheless, it seems to me that Autolus has brought a best-in-class product to market and that it has the funds both to build on an initially successful drug launch and fund studies in new indications.

Considering all of the above, I am awarding Autolus stock a 'Buy" rating before what could be a data readout from the FELIX study of real significance, paving the way for new approval shots in oncology markets, and more autoimmune data.

If Autolus can open up autoimmune markets, I can see its market cap valuation climbing >$1bn, but funding will be required, so I'd also expect Autolus to complete a fundraising if its share price does spike, which will be dilutive to investors, but shouldn't stop them securing a good return on investment.

data.title: Allspring Disciplined U.S. Core Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
FUND STRATEGY

  • Emphasizes undervalued companies that are likely to exceed earnings expectations.
  • Implements a disciplined quantitatively based strategy grounded in fundamental research; companies are evaluated based on factors related to valuation, quality, and momentum.
  • Consistently seeks to add value to the benchmark while actively managing portfolio risk using risk modeling and optimization tools.

Quarterly Review

  • The Allspring Disciplined U.S. Core Equity Fund (MUTF:EVSAX) returned -5.07% for the quarter. The benchmark S&P 500 Index (SP500, SPX) returned -4.27%.
  • For the three-month period, industrials, information technology (IT), and energy detracted from relative performance.
  • Utilities, communication services, and health care added to relative performance.

Market Review

After reaching a record high in February, the S&P 500 Index concluded a volatile first quarter of 2025 down 4.27%, snapping a streak of five consecutive quarters of positive returns for the index. During January, U.S. equities rallied on healthy corporate earnings and optimism over the pro-growth and deregulation initiatives of the Trump administration. However, by March, the S&P 500 Index fell into correction territory, as the momentum of U.S. equities quickly succumbed to volatile tariff policy headlines, persistent inflation, faltering tech leadership, softer corporate guidance, and recession fears.

Despite impending tariff concerns, U.S. inflation rates receded slightly in February. Price pressures eased modestly for energy, groceries, and housing, with annualized rates of U.S. headline and core Consumer Price Index inflation falling to 2.8% and 3.1%, respectively. The U.S. housing market remained stagnant due to limited affordability and inventory, with prospective buyers struggling to adjust to the “new normal” of mortgage rates between 6% and 7%.

In March, the Federal Open Market Committee maintained the federal funds rate at a target range of 4.25 to 4.50%. The Federal Reserve (Fed) paused for a second straight meeting following three consecutive rate cuts that began in September 2024. The Fed maintained a projection of two additional 25-basis-point (bp); 100 bps equal 1.00% rate cuts in 2025, which would lower the federal funds rate to 3.75-4.00% by the end of 2025.
