Portfolio Positioning

Quarterly Attribution and Analysis

  • Portfolio characteristics include: 130-220 holdings, a target sector weight +/- 1% relative to the benchmark, and identification of attractively valued companies with improving earnings fundamentals relative to the benchmark.

  • The fund returned -5.07% for the quarter, while the benchmark S&P 500 Index returned -4.27%. The fund’s underperformance was attributed primarily to negative stock selection within industrials and a modest underweight to energy. Positive stock selection within utilities, communication services, and health care added to performance.

  • The alpha model results were positive for the quarter, while the strategy’s positive exposures to beta, management quality, and profitability were additive to performance. These relative risk exposures were modest.

  • During the quarter, the strategy rotated into materials, health care, and real estate while reducing exposure to IT and communication services stocks. Within materials, we added defensive, lower-beta names that would be more resilient during periods of market uncertainty. Similarly, we increased holdings in lower-beta health care real estate investment trusts and health care stocks to maintain the portfolio beta close to one, following the factor’s strong performance in the first half of the quarter. Within IT, the strategy reduced exposure to companies associated with the artificial intelligence theme, and in communication services, we decreased exposure to names trading at weaker valuations.

Portfolio positioning

TRAILING 12-MONTH ATTRIBUTION ANALYSIS

  • Over the past 12 months, the fund returned 9.43% while the S&P 500 Index returned 8.25%. The outperformance was attributed primarily to positive stock selection within IT, communication services, and health care. Negative stock selection within energy, industrials, and real estate detracted from relative performance. Over the long term, broader diversification and risk controls surrounding sector weights typically result in stock selection being the main driver of performance within the strategy.

  • Regarding macroeconomic risks, the portfolio maintained tight exposure to inflation shocks and cold weather volatility conditions and geographic risks. Market expectations for rate cuts in 2025, particularly in light of softer data, inflation fears, and decreased growth expectations, peaked at around 4.80% in early January, falling approximately 15 basis points (bps; 100 bps = 1%) to 4.25% by the end of the first quarter of 2025, reflecting reduced recession odds. Considering potential risks and volatility in interest rates, the strategy maintained neutral exposure to long and short interest rates. The portfolio was also neutral to credit spreads, which fell to historic lows in the fourth quarter of 2024 but widened by nearly 60 bps during the last quarter due to volatility.

  • Our experience demonstrates that actively managing sector weights while strategically investing in relatively inexpensive companies with improving fundamentals is typically rewarded by long-term investors. We have continued to build portfolios adhering to this philosophy while recognizing that current pervasive macroeconomic risks necessitate an elevated emphasis on risk controls, particularly concerning industry exposures, secular growth, oil prices, inflation expectations, and credit risk. We still prefer an emphasis on secular value (i.e., quality and value) over risk and beta as we progress through the later phases of the economic cycle.

When reviewing the performance attribution of our portfolio, it is vital to remember that we construct our portfolio from the bottom up, one stock at a time. Each stock is included in the portfolio based on its own investment thesis. To help manage risk, we are aware of our sector and security weights, but we do not include a holding to obtain a sector distribution to resemble an index. Our exposure to any given sector is a result of our security selection process.

Portfolio positioning

OUTLOOK

  • As we enter the second quarter, the entire world awaits the Trump administration’s tariff policy to be implemented on April 2, or “Liberation Day” as President Trump has dubbed it. The uncertain policy outlook has caused businesses and individuals to adopt a wait-and-see approach to investment and consumption that has increased the risk of a recession. It is unclear if the new tariffs will incite retaliation or push trading partners to negotiate more favorable trade agreements.

  • Our expectation for the market to broaden out in 2025 has been challenged by the uncertainty raised by the prospect of a tariff war. While the mega-cap stocks are more exposed to the global economy, their earnings are at greater risk to earnings declines that would result from this trade war. Additionally, the AI theme that has driven the market higher has also been called into question as it is not clear whether U.S. Big Tech firms’ ability to monetize AI will be undermined by the threat from China or elsewhere. If AI investments are not monetized successfully in a relative short period, the generous valuation multiples of AI-related companies may need to compress. Currently, the consensus estimate is for the S&P 500 Index to generate 12% earnings-per-share growth for fiscal-year 2025. The index currently trades at a forward price/earnings of approximately 20. While this is not necessarily a bubble valuation level, its market capitalization has rarely been this high as a share of global gross domestic product.

  • Given the economic, geopolitical, and market backdrop, we anticipate volatile and range-bound markets. We will neutralize our exposures to macroeconomic and geopolitical risks where possible. While we are cautious, we will look to take advantage of market volatility to invest in companies with strong fundamentals at reasonable prices. As we monitor the macroeconomic environment, we will continue to diligently focus on company fundamentals and disciplined portfolio risk management.

The Core Consumer Price Index (CPI) is a measure that examines the weighted average of prices of a basket of consumer goods and services, but excludes food and energy. It is calculated by taking price changes for each item in the predetermined basket of goods and averaging them. Changes in the Core CPI are used to assess price changes associated with the cost of living. The CPI is one of the most frequently used indicators for identifying periods of inflation or deflation. ATTRIBUTION ANALYSIS:

Performance attribution and sector returns are calculated using the Brinson-Fachler attribution model. As such, performance attribution calculations may differ from the fund's actual investment results. Common sources of attribution dispersion from official returns include: the use of end of day holdings and prices, the exclusion of fees, and the use of myriad pricing sources to accommodate different benchmark providers.

DEFINITION OF TERMS:

Alpha measures the difference between a fund's actual returns and its expected performance given its level of risk (as measured by beta).

Beta measures fund volatility relative to general market movements. It is a standardized measure of systematic risk in comparison with a specified index. The benchmark beta is 1.00 by definition.

Downside capture ratio measures a fund’s replication of its benchmark during periods of negative returns. During periods of negative benchmark returns, an upside capture ratio greater than 100% reflects product performance greater than the benchmark and an upside capture ratio less than 100% reflects performance less than the benchmark.

Information ratio measures the consistency of excess return (return in excess of a benchmark). This value is determined by taking the annualized excess return over a benchmark (style benchmark by default) and dividing it by the standard deviation of excess return.

Price/Earnings (P/E) is the price of a share of a stock divided by earnings per share, usually calculated using the last year’s earnings.

R-squared is a measure of how similar a fund’s historical performance has been to that of the benchmark. The measure ranges from 0.0, which means that the fund’s performance bears no relationship to the performance of the index, to 1.0, which means that the fund’s performance was perfectly synchronized with the performance of the benchmark.

Sharpe ratio measures the potential reward offered by a mutual fund relative to its risk level. The ratio uses a fund's standard deviation and its excess return to determine reward per unit of risk. The higher the Sharpe ratio, the better the fund's historical risk-adjusted performance.

Standard deviation represents the degree to which an investment’s performance has varied from its average performance over a particular time period.

Tracking error measures the extent to which a manager’s performance mimics that of a benchmark. The value is the standard deviation of the difference between a fund’s performance and a benchmark’s performance.

Upside capture measures a fund’s replication of its benchmark during periods of positive returns. During periods of positive benchmark returns, an upside capture ratio greater than 100% reflects product performance greater than the benchmark and an upside capture ratio less than 100% reflects performance less than the benchmark.
